explicit_text: I was born in 1976 right here in Harlan County, at Appalachian Regional Hospital in Harlan. I've lived most of my life in this area, particularly in Cumberland, which I consider home. Harlan County has a rich coal mining history, with places like Benham and Mary Helen playing important roles. My family, including my dad and uncle, were deeply involved in the coal industry here, which shaped much of my upbringing and knowledge.

explicit_text: Benham is a significant coal camp in Harlan County where my dad worked as a coal miner for International Harvester. He started as a general laborer and eventually became the safety director before the mine shuttered. The closing of Benham's mine was a big event in the community, marking the end of an era for the town and the coal industry there. I learned a lot about Benham from my dad and uncle, who shared stories about its importance locally and regionally.

explicit_text: Cumberland is where I grew up and still live today; it’s truly home for me. Although I was born in Harlan, my family settled in Cumberland, and I attended school nearby. It’s a small community with strong ties to the coal industry and local culture.

entity: Southeast Kentucky Community and Technical College
explicit_text: I earned my associate degree from Southeast Kentucky Community and Technical College before continuing my education. While working there, I served as the agricultural services manager and was involved in community development projects, including building an aquaculture learning center in Lynch. This college was an important stepping stone in my career and helped me stay connected to the region’s agricultural and educational needs.

explicit_text: I completed my bachelor's degree in agriculture, animal science, at the University of Kentucky. UK is a prominent institution in the state and played a big role in shaping my knowledge and career path. After graduating, I eventually returned to Harlan County to work with the UK Cooperative Extension Service, which allowed me to serve my home community. The university’s influence is strong in Kentucky, and I’m proud to be an alumnus.

explicit_text: I attended Victory Road Christian Academy from grades seven through twelve. It was a small private school located in Blair, just outside Cumberland city limits. The school was an important part of my youth, providing a close-knit educational environment. Unlike public schools in Benham or Lynch, it was independent and didn’t receive support from the coal companies.

explicit_text: After International Harvester in Benham closed, my dad found work at Great Western Resources, which operated New Horizon Coal in the Mary Helen community. He continued his coal mining career there, moving from labor to management roles. Great Western Resources was part of the later chapter of coal mining in Harlan County for my family. It helped sustain us after the older mines shuttered.

explicit_text: US Steel was another major coal company in the area, and I worked on a project at a former US Steel shop building in Lynch. We converted the building into an aquaculture learning center where we raised trout using water from Mine Number Thirty. US Steel’s legacy remains in the infrastructure and history of the region, even after the mines closed. It’s part of the industrial heritage that shaped Harlan County.

explicit_text: I began working at the UK Cooperative Extension Service in February 2001 after serving as the agricultural services manager at Southeast Kentucky Community and Technical College. The Extension Service has been a great way for me to serve Harlan County, providing programs in agriculture, homemaking, and youth development like 4-H. Over the years, we’ve adapted to new technologies but continue traditional programs like canning and quilting. It’s rewarding to help the community maintain cultural and agricultural knowledge.

explicit_text: Mine Number Thirty is a former coal mine whose water we used in an aquaculture learning center project in Lynch. We raised trout there as part of educational and community development efforts. This project repurposed a former US Steel shop building and connected the mining legacy to new uses in the community. Mine Number Thirty’s water source was essential to the success of this initiative.

explicit_text: 4-H is a vital youth development program here in Harlan County that we support through the UK Cooperative Extension Service. It teaches leadership, gardening, and practical skills like hatching chicks and growing tomato plants. We have clubs in schools and the community, helping young people learn responsibility and skills that connect them to agriculture and their heritage. 4-H remains strong and important in the county’s social fabric.
